questions.question: Explain the purpose of the HAVING clause and how it differs from the WHERE clause. (3 marks)
expectedPoints: HAVING is used to filter groups of rows created by the GROUP BY clause.
It is applied after the grouping occurs.
It can use aggregate functions in its condition, unlike WHERE which operates on individual rows and cannot use aggregates.

questions.question: Given an 'Orders' table with columns (OrderID, CustomerID, OrderDate, TotalAmount), write a SQL query to find the total amount spent by each customer who has made more than 5 orders. List only the top 3 customers by total spending. (5 marks)
expectedPoints: Use GROUP BY CustomerID to group orders by each customer.
Use SUM(TotalAmount) to calculate the total spending per customer.
Use COUNT(OrderID) to count the number of orders per customer.
Apply HAVING COUNT(OrderID) > 5 to filter for customers with more than 5 orders.
Use ORDER BY SUM(TotalAmount) DESC to sort by total spending (highest first) and potentially LIMIT 3 to get the top 3. (Syntax may vary by SQL dialect).
